title: Planked Wood Sideboard
summary: A three-bay sideboard on 2x4 frame-and-panel end legs, with a plywood-divided interior, three drawers on real slides, and shaker-style doors framing hobby-stock center panels.
description:
Each end of this sideboard is its own small frame-and-panel assembly — 2x4 legs and panel-end caps surrounding inset 1x4 side panels — built inside-out, starting with the inset panel and working outward to the legs, so keep every back edge flush while the front side panel stays intentionally inset from the outer leg face. Two long 2x4 bottom supports run the full width underneath, doing nothing but keeping this sideboard from ever sagging in the middle over its long span.

**The storage interior is genuinely a three-bay layout**: plywood dividers split the case into a center drawer stack flanked by two door cabinets, and getting those dividers square before fitting the front trim matters, since the front trim has to land flush to the inside faces of the outer cabinets while overhanging the center bay evenly. Build the top from two edge-joined boards, and consider waiting to attach it until after the drawers are installed, since it's much easier to fit slides and boxes with the top off.

**Doors use a proper stile-and-rail frame around a hobby-stock or thin-plywood center panel** — real frame-and-panel construction, not a solid slab door — and the base gets a mitered moulding trim rather than a plain square edge. Drawers are built to fit their actual openings (remember most slides need about 1/2" clearance per side), installed inset 3/4" for the drawer face, with faces cut to fit and attached from inside with finish nails and glue after the drawer boxes are hung. The back panel is inset from the back edge for a cleaner reveal, and shelves go in last.

materials.name: 1/4" thick x 3-1/2" hobby stock, or 1/4" plywood or paneling (door centers)
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.part: Door rails and center panel frames

cutList.note: Cut to fit each drawer opening.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the six 2x4s: four legs at 34-1/2", four panel end caps at 10-1/2", two bottom supports at 75-1/2", two back framing rails at 77", and two back cleats at 18". All six boards are used and the fit is tight, so cut the two 77" rails and the two 75-1/2" supports first, one to a board. From the two 2x6s cut one 77" top board each. From the seven 1x4s: six side panels at 23-1/2", eight door stiles at 22-1/4", eight door rails and center panel frames at 11", two center divider trim at 18-1/8", six drawer box front/backs at 15-1/2", and drawer box sides at 16-3/4" — cut six of those, one pair per drawer, not the two the cut list shows. From the 1x2 cut two front trim rails at 75-1/2", two center sides at 22-3/8" and two center shorts at 5-5/8": buy THREE 8 ft 1x2s rather than the two listed, because each 75-1/2" rail eats a whole board and leaves under 21", which will not yield a 22-3/8" piece. Rip the 3/4" plywood into 16-3/4" strips for the 75-1/2" bottom shelf and the two 30-1/4" cabinet bay floors, and cut the 1/4" back panel 32" x 77". Leave the drawer faces and the baseboard until their own steps — both are cut to fit.

steps.title: Build the end panels
steps.body: Each end is its own small frame-and-panel assembly and it goes together inside-out: start by joining three 23-1/2" 1x4 side panels edge to edge, then cap that panel top and bottom with the 10-1/2" 2x4 end caps, then add a 34-1/2" leg to each side. Use 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, all placed on the inside faces so nothing shows on the finished ends. Keep every back edge dead flush as you work, but leave the side panel deliberately inset from the outer face of the legs — that recess is the frame-and-panel look, and if you flush it up instead the end reads as a slab. Build both ends and set them side by side to confirm they match before moving on.

steps.title: Add the bottom supports
steps.body: Attach the bottom supports with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws from the back side to hide them.

steps.title: Build the storage interior
steps.body: The 75-1/2" plywood bottom shelf runs the full span between the ends and sits on the two bottom supports; the two 30-1/4" bay floors then divide the case into a center drawer stack flanked by a door cabinet at each end. Set the two 18-1/8" center divider trim pieces into the base flush to the back, square to the shelf, and check them with a level and a square before anything gets fastened — the front trim in the next step has to land flush to the inside faces of the outer cabinets, and it cannot do that if the dividers are out. Glue and pocket-screw every joint, and measure the two door openings and the center opening once the dividers are set, because the doors and drawers are built to those real numbers.

steps.title: Add the front trim
steps.body: Run the two 75-1/2" 1x2 rails across the top and bottom of the case front, then fit the two 22-3/8" center sides and the two 5-5/8" center shorts around the center drawer bay. Keep everything square and straight — this trim is what the drawer faces will be gapped against, so a bowed rail shows up three times over. The center trim sits flush to the inside faces of the two side cabinets while overhanging the center bay evenly on each side. Glue and 1-1/4" brads, and clamp rather than relying on nail pressure to pull a bow out.

steps.title: Build and attach the top
steps.body: Edge-join the two top boards. Attach the top now, or wait until after the drawers are installed for easier access.

steps.title: Add the baseboard
steps.body: Cut the base moulding to fit the bottom of the case, mitering the outside corners at 45° so the profile wraps continuously — measure each run off the assembled cabinet rather than off the cut list, since the finished width depends on how your end panels came out. Dry-fit the whole run before nailing anything: a mitered corner that does not close cannot be fixed after the glue is in. Glue the miters and set the moulding with brad nails.

steps.title: Build the doors
steps.body: Build the door frames from the stiles and rails, then attach the hobby stock or thin plywood centers to complete the doors.
materials: 1/4" thick x 3-1/2" hobby stock, or 1/4" plywood or paneling (door centers)

steps.title: Build and install the drawers
steps.body: Measure each of the three center-bay openings for real rather than working from the cut list, then build each box to the opening less the slide clearance — most side-mount slides want about 1/2" per side, so a box roughly 1" narrower than its opening. Use the 15-1/2" front/back pieces with 16-3/4" sides, and check each box across both diagonals. Mount the 15" slides set back 3/4" from the front of the case so there is room for the drawer face to sit flush with the trim. Hang the boxes, then cut each 5-1/2" wide face to fit its own opening from the 1x6 — cut them last and expect to trim the third, since three 24" faces use the full 6 ft board with nothing left for saw kerf. Fix the faces from inside the box with glue and 1-1/4" brads once you have shimmed an even gap all round.

steps.title: Attach the back and shelves
steps.body: Attach the back panel, inset from the back edge for a clean reveal, then add the interior shelves.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, apply primer and paint or stain and poly, and add the drawer and door pulls.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Planked wood sideboard with three drawers and two paneled doors
